question: Has the 'Venus de Milo' been replaced by a statue clearly recognizable as the 'Winged Victory of Samothrace' (Nike of Samothrace)?
Does the new statue feature large, dramatic outstretched wings, which are the defining characteristic of the Winged Victory?
Is the new statue depicted without a head and without arms, consistent with the historical state of the Winged Victory?
Does the statue exhibit wind-blown, clinging drapery and a forward-leaning dynamic pose (as opposed to the static pose of Venus)?
Has the text on the metal floor plaque been updated to read 'Winged Victory of Samothrace' (or 'Nike of Samothrace')?
Does the updated plaque maintain the original typographic style, layout, and bilingual format (if originally present)?
Is the new statue correctly positioned on the central pedestal where the Venus de Milo originally stood?
Do the highlights and shadows on the new statue (especially the wings) align with the room's existing spotlight direction?
Are the cast shadows on the floor or wall consistent with the shape of the winged figure?
Is the marble texture of the new statue consistent with the visual aesthetic and material of the original museum collection?
Are the two oil paintings on the left wall preserved exactly as they were in the original image?
Are the tourists, the gallery guide, and their clothing/poses preserved without distortion or alteration?
Is the velvet rope barrier and its stanchions preserved intact?
Is the wall text 'CLASSICAL SCULPTURE AND THE IDEAL HUMAN BODY' preserved unchanged?
Is the camera angle and perspective maintained exactly as in the original image?
Is the new statue integrated into the scene without visible seams, halos, or masking artifacts?
Are the proportions of the Winged Victory appropriate for the pedestal and the surrounding gallery space?
Is the image free from generative artifacts (e.g., melted geometry, blurred details, or illogical structural distortions)?
